Reference: RFC 10008 — The HTTP QUERY Method, IETF, June 2026.
The last time the global web architecture received a major new HTTP method was PATCH back in 2010.
The IETF published RFC 10008 this month, standardizing a new HTTP method called QUERY. It gives API designers a formal way to send large, structured, read-only requests without reaching for POST.
Since then, modern data requirements have ballooned. The rise of GraphQL, massive JSON filtering parameters, and deep search analytics have pushed standard HTTP protocols to their breaking points.
To bridge this structural gap, the IETF officially standardized the QUERY method in RFC 10008. It completely alters how we approach data-retrieval operations - while simultaneously uncovering a highly volatile attack surface for web application security.
The Broken Compromise: Why GET and POST Fall Short
When fetching data from an endpoint with sophisticated search filters, engineering teams have historically been forced to choose between two fundamentally flawed implementations.
1. The GET Bottleneck
The REST paradigm dictates that reading data requires a GET request. However, when those search parameters include complex objects, nested arrays, or JSONPath strings, URL query parameters break down:

• Size Limitations: Intermediary proxies, CDNs, and browsers frequently cap URLs at roughly 8,000 characters. Pass anything larger, and the request drops.

• Log Exposure: Anything inside a URL query parameter gets written directly to cleartext server access logs, upstream SIEM systems, browser histories, and Referer headers.

• Encoding Friction: Forcing multi-layered relational database filters into URL-encoded format is clunky and highly prone to parser errors.
2. The POST Hack
To bypass URL limitations, developers routinely build POST endpoints solely for data retrieval. While this successfully shifts the payload into the request body, it breaks the very core of HTTP mechanics:

• Zero Idempotence: POST is structurally non-idempotent. If a network connection drops mid-flight, an automatic client retry risks executing a duplicate state-changing operation or throwing server exceptions.

• Broken Caching: Downstream edge networks, CDNs, and browser caches will not cache a POST response natively. This forces engineers to build custom, complex caching abstractions inside application code.
Enter the QUERY Method
The QUERY method acts as a pristine hybrid: it delivers the read-only, cacheable semantics of a GET request paired with the payload-carrying capabilities of a POST request.
Here is a look at an RFC-compliant QUERY request in action:
HTTP
QUERY /api/v1/orders HTTP/1.1
Host: data-warehouse.local
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json{
"select": ["order_id", "status", "payout"],
"filter": {
"payout_status": "settled",
"amount": {"$gt": 50000}
},
"limit": 50
}
Core Architecture Rules Under RFC 10008

• Explicitly Safe and Idempotent: QUERY is strictly read-only. Calling it once yields the exact same state effect as calling it 1,000 times. Clients can automatically replay dropped queries without warning flags.

• Cacheable Bodies: Edge caches can now use the request body as part of the primary cache key to store data-heavy search results efficiently.

• The Request URI Mapping: Servers can reply to a QUERY with a Location header containing a temporary tracking URL (e.g., /saved-queries/99). A downstream client can then simply perform a traditional GET against /saved-queries/99 to re-trigger that identical payload logic later on.
How HTTP Verbs Stack Up

• Safe (Read-Only): GET (Yes) | POST (No) | QUERY (Yes)

• Idempotent: GET (Yes) | POST (No) | QUERY (Yes)

• Allows Request Body: GET (No defined semantics) | POST (Yes) | QUERY (Yes)

• Cacheable: GET (Yes) | POST (Complex/Non-Standard) | QUERY (Yes)

• Safely Replayable: GET (Yes) | POST (No) | QUERY (Yes)
The Threat Landscape: Web Cache Poisoning in a QUERY World
Whenever the fundamental plumbing of the web updates, security boundaries shift. For bug bounty hunters and penetration testers, QUERY introduces a fertile landscape for Web Cache Poisoning due to parser desynchronization.
Attack Vector A: The “Ignored Body” Poisoning Flaw
During the early years of enterprise adoption, many legacy reverse proxies and CDNs will recognize QUERY as an idempotent method akin to GET, but their hashing mechanisms will still calculate the cache key using only the Host header and URI Path — completely ignoring the payload body.
An attacker can transmit a QUERY request containing a malicious XSS payload inside the body:
HTTP
QUERY /search HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable-target.com
Content-Type: application/json{"user_profile": "<script>alert(origin)</script>"}
If the application server reflects this input into the response while an un-updated edge cache saves the response using only the cache key [vulnerable-target.com/search](https://vulnerable-target.com/search), the cache becomes thoroughly poisoned. Every subsequent visitor executing a completely clean search against /search will be served the attacker's cached script payload.
Attack Vector B: Parameter Pollution & JSON Desync
If the edge cache does include the request body in its hashing routine, vulnerabilities can still emerge from variations in how different JSON engines process duplicated keys.
Consider an attacker passing duplicate keys to an endpoint:
HTTP
QUERY /api/users HTTP/1.1
Host: vulnerable-target.com
Content-Type: application/json{
"search": "legitimate_term",
"search": "malicious_payload\"><script>..."
}
If the edge cache’s parsing engine keys off the first occurrence of a duplicate key (legitimate_term), but the backend application server utilizes an engine that overrides and processes the last occurrence, a desynchronization happens. The poisoned response containing the executed exploit gets mapped squarely against the completely clean legitimate_term cache key.
Auditing QUERY Endpoints: A Hands-On Guide for Burp Suite
To effectively map out and exploit these endpoints during a security assessment, you need to configure your testing stack to handle custom HTTP methods cleanly.
Step 1: Configure Global Content-Length Rules in Repeater
Navigate to the top-level menu in Burp Suite and select Repeater. Ensure that Update Content-Length is checked. Because QUERY relies completely on an accurate body size definition, manual editing without automatic calculation will cause your packets to hang or get rejected with 400 Bad Request responses.
Step 2: Implement Dynamic Discovery via Proxy Rewriting
You can passively discover undocumented QUERY support while browsing a target:

• Go to Proxy > Proxy settings > Match and Replace.

• Click Add and set the Type to Request first line.

• Use the regex Match rule: ^GET (.*) HTTP/1\.1$

• Set the Replace string to: QUERY $1 HTTP/1.1
As you navigate the target application normally, Burp will rewrite your outbound traffic on the fly. Monitor your HTTP history logs for 200 OK responses to uncover legacy or undocumented support for the method.
Step 3: Execute Body-Key Verification in Intruder
When actively fuzzing a confirmed QUERY endpoint:

• Send the request to Intruder and define your payload markers directly inside the JSON structure in the body.

• Navigate to Payloads > Payload Encoding and uncheck URL-encode these characters to prevent Burp from destroying valid JSON notation.

• Inject cache-busting tokens directly inside the body payload structure to observe whether the upstream edge server responds with cache misses (X-Cache: MISS) or cache hits (X-Cache: HIT).
The Road Ahead
If you are deploying support for QUERY across internal APIs, you must ensure your Web Application Firewall (WAF) layers are updated to inspect request bodies on QUERY verbs with the same depth they apply to POST packets. Furthermore, your edge cache keys must explicitly enforce uniform body parsing to eliminate normalization discrepancies.
The adoption curve for RFC 10008 will scale rapidly over the coming months. Understanding its underlying mechanics ensures your applications can harness the speed of a cacheable, heavy-duty read structure without dropping your guard to advanced edge-routing exploits.
